steps.title: Identify what the blank needs
steps.content: The passage builds a causal chain: electrons can't reach the electrode efficiently because they must pass through slow redox reactions, capping power at 0.30 mW/cm². Silver nanoparticles raised that to 0.66 mW/cm². The blank must complete the researchers' hypothesis — an explanation that uses silver's high electrical conductivity to account for the doubled power output.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'silver nanoparticles may increase the metabolic processes of the bacteria': The problem is electron transport, not bacterial metabolism. Metabolism produces the electrons; the bottleneck is getting them to the electrode. ✗
  • Choice B — 'redox reactions may accelerate independent of the presence of the silver nanoparticles': This negates the silver's role entirely, which contradicts the experiment's design and the conductivity premise. ✗
  • Choice C — 'silver nanoparticles may allow electrons to bypass the series of redox reactions and transfer directly to the electrode': Conductivity means electrons move easily through silver rather than through the slow redox chain. This directly explains the power increase using the stated premise. ✓
  • Choice D — 'electrons may be conducted directly to the electrode before the silver nanoparticles catalyze the redox reactions': This implies silver catalyzes the very reactions it should be bypassing, and the timing claim ("before") is unsupported. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
